---
title: "Economy & Jobs"
description: "GDP, productivity, and jobs — Canada and OECD peers"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import (
    CANADA_COLOR, PEER_COLOR, HIGHLIGHT_WIDTH, PEER_WIDTH,
    HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY, PEER_COUNTRIES, get_data_date,
)
from pipeline.charts import (
    peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar,
    peer_comparison_line_by_age, ranked_bar_by_age,
)

# Age brackets for the labour-market charts (display order; total shown first).
LABOUR_AGES = ["Total (15–64)", "Youth (15–24)", "Prime-age (25–54)", "Older (55–64)"]
```

How fast the economy is growing, how large it is per person, how productive an
hour of work is, and how many people who want work can find it — these indicators
place Canada's output and labour market alongside 17 OECD peers. Inflation and the
cost of living have their own page: [Housing & Cost of Living](../housing/).

## Where Canada stands

A scorecard of where Canada ranks among the 17 peers on each measure (latest available year). Dots are ordered by rank, so **further right is more favourable for Canada** (lower-is-better measures like unemployment are flipped accordingly). Canada is red, key comparators in colour; hover a dot for its value and rank.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import page_snapshot
page_snapshot("economics").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Real GDP growth

Annual percentage change in real (inflation-adjusted) GDP — the headline measure of how fast the economy is expanding or contracting. The most recent year or two are OECD projections rather than final figures.

```{python}
#| echo: false
gdpg_path = "data/economics/oecd_real_gdp_growth.csv"
if os.path.exists(gdpg_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(gdpg_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "real_gdp_growth",
        title="", yaxis_title="Real GDP growth (annual %)", show_average=True,
        initial_start=2015)
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dot", line_color="#888", opacity=0.5)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(gdpg_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Real GDP growth data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Quarterly GDP growth

Real (inflation-adjusted) gross domestic product, shown as the change from the previous quarter, for every quarter back to 1961. Red bars mark quarters in which the economy contracted. A **recession** is commonly defined as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP, though in Canada the official arbiter is the C.D. Howe Institute's Business Cycle Council; the recessions it has dated since 1980 are marked here as shaded bands. The full record places recent quarters alongside past downturns — the contractions of the early 1980s and early 1990s, the 2008–09 financial crisis, and the record swings of the 2020 pandemic. (Statistics Canada's published "% change" is this quarter-over-quarter measure; the figure often quoted in the news — "*X*% annualized" — is the same change compounded to a yearly rate.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
gdpq_path = "data/economics/statcan_gdp_quarterly.csv"
if os.path.exists(gdpq_path):
    q = pd.read_csv(gdpq_path, parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values("date")
    q["growth"] = q["gdp"].pct_change() * 100
    q = q.dropna(subset=["growth"]).copy()
    q["qlabel"] = q["date"].dt.year.astype(str) + " Q" + q["date"].dt.quarter.astype(str)
    # Pre-format the hover text in Python: Quarto's bundled Plotly does not reliably
    # honour a d3 number-format inside hovertemplate (same family as the window.Plotly
    # quirk), so bake the rounded value into the string. <0.05 rounds to a clean "0.0%"
    # (avoids a "-0.0%" for near-flat quarters).
    q["hover"] = [f"{lbl}: {'0.0' if abs(g) < 0.05 else format(g, '+.1f')}%"
                  for lbl, g in zip(q["qlabel"], q["growth"])]
    bar_colors = ["#d62728" if v < 0 else "#4e79a7" for v in q["growth"]]
    import math
    from pipeline.charts import add_recession_bands
    # Clip the y-axis to normal quarters so past recessions stay legible; the
    # 2020 pandemic swings (≈ −11% / +9%) are ~5× any other quarter and would
    # otherwise crush the whole series into a flat band near zero.
    _normal = q.loc[q["growth"].abs() <= 5, "growth"]
    _ylo, _yhi = math.floor(_normal.min()) - 0.5, math.ceil(_normal.max()) + 0.5
    # Open on the most recent ~10 years — recent trends draw the most attention
    # for this chart; the slider still spans the full 1961– record and "All" resets
    # it. The ±2-month pad keeps the first/last quarterly bars from clipping.
    _xmax = q["date"].max()
    _xlo = _xmax - pd.DateOffset(years=10, months=2)
    _xhi = _xmax + pd.DateOffset(months=2)
    figq = go.Figure(go.Bar(
        x=q["date"], y=q["growth"], marker_color=bar_colors,
        customdata=q["hover"],
        hovertemplate="%{customdata}<extra></extra>"))
    figq.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1)
    # Shade the C.D. Howe Business Cycle Council's dated recessions behind the bars
    # (neutral grey, below the data) — visible once the slider is zoomed out past 2008.
    add_recession_bands(figq)
    figq.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", showlegend=False, height=460, bargap=0.15,
        yaxis=dict(title="Quarter-over-quarter % change",
                   gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%", zeroline=False,
                   range=[_ylo, _yhi]),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", range=[_xlo, _xhi],
                   rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                       dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                       dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                       dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120))
    figq.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 36-10-0104-01 (real GDP, seasonally adjusted); recession bands: C.D. Howe Institute Business Cycle Council | Data as of: {q['qlabel'].iloc[-1]}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    if (q["growth"].abs() > 5).any():
        figq.add_annotation(x="2020-07-01", y=_yhi, yanchor="top",
            text="2020 pandemic<br>quarters (off-scale)",
            showarrow=False, align="center", font=dict(size=9, color="#999"))
    figq.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Quarterly GDP data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## GDP per capita (PPP, current USD)

GDP per capita at purchasing power parity allows meaningful comparison of living standards across countries. Canada is shown in red; the United States and other key comparators are highlighted in colour, with the remaining peers in light grey.

```{python}
#| echo: false
gdppc_path = "data/economics/oecd_gdp_per_capita.csv"
if os.path.exists(gdppc_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(gdppc_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "gdp_per_capita",
        title="", yaxis_title="USD (PPP)", show_average=True, initial_start=2015)
    fig.update_layout(yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f", annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD National Accounts | Data as of: {get_data_date(gdppc_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("GDP per capita data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest GDP per capita: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(gdppc_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(gdppc_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "gdp_per_capita", "USD (PPP)",
        f"Source: OECD National Accounts | Data as of: {get_data_date(gdppc_path)}",
        tickformat="$,.0f", hover_template="%{y}: $%{x:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## GDP per capita relative to the US and peers

One of the most-discussed economic questions is whether Canadian living standards are slipping behind. This chart answers it directly: Canada's GDP per capita (PPP) shown **as a percentage of the United States** and **of the peer-group average** (the other 16 OECD economies), over time. A value of 100% means parity; below 100% means Canada trails. We compare against the full peer group rather than the narrower G7, with the United States shown as a single named comparator.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import lines_over_time

if os.path.exists(gdppc_path):
    g = pd.read_csv(gdppc_path)
    piv = g.pivot(index="year", columns="country_code", values="gdp_per_capita")
    peers_ex_ca = [c for c in piv.columns if c != HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY]
    def _rel(denom, label):
        s = (100 * piv[HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY] / denom).rename("value").reset_index()
        s["comparator"] = label
        return s
    rel = pd.concat([
        _rel(piv["USA"], "vs. the United States"),
        _rel(piv[peers_ex_ca].mean(axis=1), "vs. the peer-group average"),
    ], ignore_index=True).dropna(subset=["value"])
    fig = lines_over_time(
        rel, "year", "value", "comparator",
        yaxis_title="Canada's GDP per capita (% of comparator)",
        colors={"vs. the United States": "#1f77b4",
                "vs. the peer-group average": "#555555"},
        group_order=["vs. the United States", "vs. the peer-group average"],
        yticksuffix="%", hover_decimals=1, rangemode="normal",
        source_note=f"Source: OECD National Accounts (GDP per capita, PPP) | Data as of: {get_data_date(gdppc_path)}",
    )
    # Span the full data: early years sit above 100% (Canada once led the peer
    # average), so the top must clear the series max, not a hard 102.
    _lo, _hi = rel["value"].min(), rel["value"].max()
    fig.update_yaxes(range=[max(0, _lo - 3), max(102, _hi + 2)])
    fig.add_hline(y=100, line_dash="dot", line_color="#999",
                  annotation_text="parity (100%)", annotation_position="top right")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("GDP per capita data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Labour productivity (GDP per hour worked)

GDP per hour worked measures how efficiently an economy converts labour into output. Canada's productivity gap relative to the United States and other leading economies is a persistent concern.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import peer_comparison_line
import os

prod_path = "data/economics/oecd_labour_productivity.csv"
if os.path.exists(prod_path):
    prod = pd.read_csv(prod_path)

    fig = peer_comparison_line(
        prod, x_col="year", y_col="gdp_per_hour",
        title="", yaxis_title="USD (PPP) per hour worked",
    )
    fig.update_layout(
        yaxis_tickformat="$,.1f",
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Source: OECD Productivity Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(prod_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
            showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
        )],
    )
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Labour productivity data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Latest labour productivity: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(prod_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(prod_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "gdp_per_hour", "USD (PPP) per hour worked",
        f"Source: OECD Productivity Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(prod_path)}",
        tickformat="$,.0f", hover_template="%{y}: $%{x:.1f}/hr<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Unemployment rate

Harmonised unemployment rate as a percentage of the labour force, allowing consistent comparison across countries. **Use the dropdown to switch age bracket** — youth (15–24) unemployment runs far above the prime-age rate, and the peer ranking shifts with it.

```{python}
#| echo: false
unemp_path = "data/economics/oecd_unemployment_by_age.csv"
if os.path.exists(unemp_path):
    unemp = pd.read_csv(unemp_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line_by_age(
        unemp, x_col="year", y_col="unemployment_rate", age_col="age",
        yaxis_title="% of labour force", ages=LABOUR_AGES, default_age=LABOUR_AGES[0])
    fig.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: OECD Labour Force Statistics (harmonised, by age) | Data as of: {get_data_date(unemp_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Unemployment-by-age data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Latest unemployment rate: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(unemp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(unemp_path)
    fig = ranked_bar_by_age(df, "unemployment_rate", "age", "% of labour force",
        "Source: OECD Labour Force Statistics (harmonised, by age)",
        ages=LABOUR_AGES, default_age=LABOUR_AGES[0],
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Unemployment by city

The national rate hides large regional differences. This map shows the **current** unemployment rate in each of Canada's largest metropolitan areas — **zoom and pan to your area.** From the monthly Labour Force Survey (three-month moving average, seasonally adjusted), so it stays current with the national series above; it covers the ~40 largest CMAs (smaller centres aren't separately estimated by the LFS).

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_map
cma_geo_path = "data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"
cma_unemp_path = "data/economics/statcan_cma_unemployment.csv"
if os.path.exists(cma_geo_path) and os.path.exists(cma_unemp_path):
    cma_geo = json.load(open(cma_geo_path))
    sub = pd.read_csv(cma_unemp_path, dtype={"cmauid": str}).dropna(subset=["unemployment"])
    period = pd.to_datetime(str(sub["period"].iloc[0]) + "-01").strftime("%b %Y")
    fig = choropleth_map(cma_geo, sub, "cmauid", "unemployment", name_col="name",
        colorbar_title="Unemployment<br>rate", value_suffix="%", value_fmt=".1f", colorscale="YlOrRd",
        zmin=float(sub["unemployment"].quantile(0.05)), zmax=float(sub["unemployment"].quantile(0.95)),
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada Labour Force Survey 14-10-0459-01 (3-month moving average, seasonally adjusted) | 3 months ending {period}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("City unemployment map not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Employment rate

The share of an age group that is employed. It complements the unemployment rate by capturing people who are neither working nor counted as unemployed — for instance those who have stopped looking for work. **Use the dropdown to switch age bracket:** older-worker (55–64) employment, in particular, has trended up across most peers.

```{python}
#| echo: false
emp_path = "data/economics/oecd_employment_by_age.csv"
if os.path.exists(emp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(emp_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line_by_age(
        df, x_col="year", y_col="employment_rate", age_col="age",
        yaxis_title="% of population in age group",
        ages=LABOUR_AGES, default_age=LABOUR_AGES[0], show_average=True)
    fig.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: OECD Labour Force Statistics (harmonised, by age) | Data as of: {get_data_date(emp_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Employment rate data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest employment rate: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(emp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(emp_path)
    fig = ranked_bar_by_age(df, "employment_rate", "age", "% of population in age group",
        "Source: OECD Labour Force Statistics (harmonised, by age)",
        ages=LABOUR_AGES, default_age=LABOUR_AGES[0],
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Business investment

Gross fixed capital formation — spending on machinery, equipment, buildings, and intellectual property — as a share of GDP. Investment is what builds the capital each worker has to work with, so it is closely tied to the productivity and GDP-per-capita pictures above: persistently weak investment is one of the most common explanations for weak productivity growth.

```{python}
#| echo: false
inv_path = "data/economics/worldbank_business_investment.csv"
if os.path.exists(inv_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(inv_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "investment_pct_gdp",
        title="", yaxis_title="% of GDP", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: World Bank / OECD National Accounts | Data as of: {get_data_date(inv_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Business investment data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest business investment: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(inv_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(inv_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "investment_pct_gdp", "% of GDP",
        f"Source: World Bank / OECD National Accounts | Data as of: {get_data_date(inv_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Current account balance

Canada's current account balance as a share of GDP — the broadest measure of its trade and income position with the rest of the world. Below zero is a deficit (the country spends and invests more than it earns abroad, financed by borrowing or selling assets to foreigners); above zero is a surplus.

```{python}
#| echo: false
ca_path = "data/economics/oecd_current_account.csv"
if os.path.exists(ca_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(ca_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "current_account",
        title="", yaxis_title="% of GDP", show_average=True)
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.5)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(ca_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Current account data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest current account balance: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(ca_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(ca_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "current_account", "% of GDP",
        f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook | Data as of: {get_data_date(ca_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Trade exposure to the United States

The United States takes about two-thirds of Canada's merchandise exports — a share that peaked near 88% around 2000, drifted down over the following two decades, and dropped sharply during the 2025 tariff conflict. Shown for scale, the **European Union** and **China** — Canada's next-largest export markets — each account for only about 5–6%, a measure of how concentrated Canada's trade is on a single partner. (Customs basis, seasonally adjusted.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
trade_path = "data/economics/statcan_trade_us.csv"
if os.path.exists(trade_path):
    tr = pd.read_csv(trade_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fige = go.Figure()
    for col, name, clr, w in [
        ("exports_us_share", "United States", "#1f77b4", 3),
        ("exports_eu_share", "European Union", "#59a14f", 2),
        ("exports_china_share", "China", "#f28e2b", 2)]:
        fige.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=tr["date"], y=tr[col], name=name, mode="lines",
            line=dict(color=clr, width=w), hovertemplate=name + ": %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    fige.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Share of total exports (%)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            ticksuffix="%", rangemode="tozero"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        legend=dict(yanchor="middle", y=0.5, xanchor="left", x=0.02, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120, r=20))
    fige.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 12-10-0011-01 (Customs basis, seasonally adjusted) | Data as of: {get_data_date(trade_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    fige.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Trade data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Trade balance: the US vs. the rest of the world

Canada runs a large merchandise **surplus with the United States** and a **deficit with every other market combined** — so the overall trade balance hinges on the US relationship. (Monthly, seasonally adjusted; balance = exports − imports.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(trade_path):
    tr = pd.read_csv(trade_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    figb = go.Figure()
    figb.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1)
    figb.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=tr["date"], y=tr["balance_us"] / 1000, name="With the United States",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color="#1f77b4", width=2), hovertemplate="US: $%{y:.1f}B<extra></extra>"))
    figb.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=tr["date"], y=tr["balance_row"] / 1000, name="With the rest of the world",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color="#888888", width=2), hovertemplate="Rest of world: $%{y:.1f}B<extra></extra>"))
    figb.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=440, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Monthly trade balance ($ billions)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", tickprefix="$", zeroline=False),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            range=[pd.Timestamp("2005-01-01"), tr["date"].max()],
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        legend=dict(yanchor="top", y=0.98, xanchor="left", x=0.02, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120, r=20))
    figb.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 12-10-0011-01 (Customs basis, SA; balance = exports − imports) | Data as of: {get_data_date(trade_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figb.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## The Canadian dollar

The exchange rate against the **US dollar** — Canada's dominant trading partner (see the trade-exposure chart above). Shown as the Bank of Canada publishes it: **Canadian dollars per US dollar**, so a *higher* line means a *weaker* loonie. The daily average series begins in 2017.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import single_line
fx_path = "data/economics/boc_usdcad.csv"
if os.path.exists(fx_path):
    fx = pd.read_csv(fx_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(fx, "date", "usdcad", title="",
        yaxis_title="Canadian dollars per US dollar", rangeslider=True,
        yaxis_tickformat=".2f",
        hovertemplate="%{x|%b %Y}: C$%{y:.4f} per US$<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Bank of Canada (daily average exchange rate) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fx_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Exchange-rate data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Real GDP Growth (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_real_gdp_growth.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [GDP per Capita (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_gdp_per_capita.csv) — OECD National Accounts, PPP current USD
- [Labour Productivity (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_labour_productivity.csv) — OECD Productivity Database
- [Unemployment Rate by age (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_unemployment_by_age.csv) — OECD Labour Force Statistics
- [Employment Rate by age (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_employment_by_age.csv) — OECD Labour Force Statistics
- [Business Investment (CSV)](../data/economics/worldbank_business_investment.csv) — World Bank / OECD National Accounts
- [Current Account Balance (CSV)](../data/economics/oecd_current_account.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Merchandise Trade with the US (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_trade_us.csv) — Statistics Canada 12-10-0011-01
- [CAD/US dollar exchange rate (CSV)](../data/economics/boc_usdcad.csv) — Bank of Canada
